exercise.insights.journey.trend.improvin: You’re improving — your recent accuracy is higher than your all-time average.
exercise.insights.journey.trend.insuffic: Answer a few more days of questions to see how your accuracy is trending.
exercise.insights.journey.trend.steady: Your accuracy has been steady — keep the momentum going.
exercise.insights.recommendation.habit.i: Add a little more practice time this week.
exercise.insights.recommendation.habit.p: Slow down slightly and protect your accuracy.
exercise.insights.recommendation.habit.r: Rebuild a small daily habit before pushing for speed.
exercise.insights.stage.next.complete: You’ve unlocked all dimensions — keep drilling to master them all.
exercise.insights.strengths.none: Keep going — your strengths will reveal themselves soon.

exercise.insights.title: Learning insights
explanation.form-i-pattern.a-a: Use the past fatḥa ({pastForm}), present fatḥa ({presentForm}) pattern for this verb. Common for transitive physical action. Memorize the vowel class with each Form I verb.
explanation.form-i-pattern.a-i: Use the past fatḥa ({pastForm}), present kasra ({presentForm}) pattern for this verb. Common for sitting, dwelling, and intransitive motion. Memorize the vowel class with each Form I verb.
explanation.form-i-pattern.a-u: Use the past fatḥa ({pastForm}), present ḍamma ({presentForm}) pattern for this verb. Roots with ح، ع، غ، خ often follow this pattern. However, there are many exceptions, so the vowel class for Form I verbs must be memorized.
explanation.form-i-pattern.i-a: Use the past kasra ({pastForm}), present fatḥa ({presentForm}) pattern for this verb. Often for stative or emotional verbs. Memorize the vowel class with each Form I verb.
explanation.form-i-pattern.i-i: Use the past kasra ({pastForm}), present kasra ({presentForm}) pattern for this verb. Memorize the vowel class with each Form I verb.
explanation.form-i-pattern.i-u: Use the past kasra ({pastForm}), present ḍamma ({presentForm}) pattern for this verb. This combination is very rare. Memorize the vowel class with each Form I verb.
explanation.form-i-pattern.u-a: Use the past ḍamma ({pastForm}), present fatḥa ({presentForm}) pattern for this verb. This combination is extremely rare. Memorize the vowel class with each Form I verb.
explanation.form-i-pattern.u-i: Use the past ḍamma ({pastForm}), present kasra ({presentForm}) pattern for this verb. This combination is extremely rare. Memorize the vowel class with each Form I verb.
explanation.form-i-pattern.u-u: Use the past ḍamma ({pastForm}), present ḍamma ({presentForm}) pattern for this verb. Often for qualities or states. Memorize the vowel class with each Form I verb.
explanation.form-root.assimilation-compl: In Form VIII: if the first radical is د، ذ، ث، ط, or ظ, expect the تَ infix to assimilate completely — a doubled consonant appears in place of تَ.
explanation.form-root.assimilation-empha: In Form VIII: if the first radical is ص or ض, expect the تَ infix to shift to emphatic طَ.
explanation.form-root.assimilation-voici: In Form VIII: if the first radical is ز, expect the تَ infix to change to voiced دَ (e.g., اِزْدَوَجَ).
explanation.form.1-action: When the past vowel is fatḥa, expect physical or external action directed at an object or space — the broadest Form I class. The specific present-stem vowel still needs to be memorized with each verb.
explanation.form.1-intermediate: When the past vowel is kasra, expect cognitive or emotional meaning — halfway between action and state.
explanation.form.1-stative: When the past vowel is ḍamma, expect an inherent quality or state — imperative forms are rarely meaningful here.
explanation.form.10: Notice the اِسْتَـ prefix (اِسْتَفْعَلَ) — it typically means to seek, consider, or request the base Form I action.
explanation.form.2: Notice the doubled middle consonant — that gemination is the Form II marker, typically adding intensity or a causative sense.
explanation.form.3: Notice the long vowel after the first radical (فَاعَلَ) — that lengthening marks Form III and signals acting with or toward another.
explanation.form.4: Notice the initial hamza (أَفْعَلَ) — that prefix is the Form IV marker, typically introducing a causative or declarative meaning.
explanation.form.5: Look for the ت prefix and the doubled middle radical (تَفَعَّلَ) — together they mark Form V, usually reflexive or inchoative.
explanation.form.6: Look for the ت prefix and the long vowel after the first radical (تَفَاعَلَ) — that pair marks Form VI, signalling reciprocal action.
explanation.form.7: Notice the اِنْ prefix (اِنْفَعَلَ) — it converts a Form I action into an inchoative or passive/reflexive one.
explanation.form.8: Notice the ت inserted after the first radical (اِفْتَعَلَ) — that infix is the Form VIII marker, giving an internal or reflexive sense. The initial اِ is hamzat al-wasl: it supports the opening consonant cluster when the verb stands alone, and drops whenever a preceding word ends in a vowel.
explanation.form.9: Notice the initial alif and the doubled final radical (اِفْعَلَّ) — both together mark Form IX, used for colors and bodily defects.
explanation.form.1q: The base pattern for four-consonant roots is فَعْلَلَ (Form Iq). All four radicals together carry the core meaning.
explanation.form.1q-bd: A biliteral root repeated twice gives the فَعفَعَ pattern (Form Iq). The repetition itself signals intensity or repeated action.
explanation.form.2q: Look for the تَ prefix on Form Iq (تَفَعْلَلَ) — that marks Form IIq, typically reflexive or inchoative for four-consonant roots.
explanation.form.3q: Notice the اِ prefix and the نـ infix between the second and third radicals (اِفْعَنْلَلَ) — Form IIIq, reflexive or passive, and relatively rare.
explanation.form.4q: Notice the اِ prefix and the doubled final two radicals (اِفْعَلَلَّ) — Form IVq, for colors or physical states; very rare.
explanation.nominal.activeParticiple: To identify the active participle (اسم الفاعل): in Forms II–X, look for the مُـ prefix; in Form I, expect the فَاعِل pattern.
explanation.nominal.activeParticiple.for: This verb’s Form I active participle is lexical, not the regular فَاعِل pattern. Memorize it alongside the verb.

A common Form I lexical pattern is فَعِيل, an adjective for a fixed attribute, especially a quality seen as part of someone’s nature. It usually follows the meaning of the verb, but you should still memorize it.
explanation.nominal.activeParticiple.qua: To identify the active participle (اسم الفاعل) of a quadriliteral verb, look for the مُـ prefix — always present. In Form Iq, the pattern is مُفَعْلِل.
explanation.nominal.masdar.form-i: In Form I, the masdar is lexical — memorize it alongside the verb. Common patterns as anchors: فَعْل (momentary action), فُعُول (motion), فِعَالَة (craft) — but these are tendencies, not rules.
explanation.nominal.masdar.form-i-mimi: This Form I mīmī masdar follows the pattern {pattern}. A mīmī masdar starts with مـ and names the action; because it can look like a noun of time or place, use context to confirm it is a masdar here.
explanation.nominal.masdar.1q: Apply the regular فَعْلَلَة pattern to get the Form Iq masdar — no separate memorization needed, unlike the triliteral Form I masdar.
explanation.nominal.masdar.1q-bd: Apply the regular فَعْلَلَة pattern to get the Form Iq masdar — no separate memorization needed, unlike the triliteral Form I masdar.
explanation.nominal.masdar.2q: Apply the تَفَعْلُل pattern to get the Form IIq masdar — the action noun for the reflexive or inchoative quadriliteral.
explanation.nominal.masdar.3q: Apply the اِفْعِنْلَال pattern to get the Form IIIq masdar — reflexive or passive action of a transitive quadriliteral. Form IIIq is rare.
explanation.nominal.masdar.4q: Apply the اِفْعِلَّال pattern to get the Form IVq masdar — colors or physical states. Form IVq is very rare.
explanation.nominal.masdar.non-form-i: To form the Form {form} masdar: apply the pattern {pattern} to the root.
explanation.nominal.passiveParticiple: To identify the passive participle (اسم المفعول): in Forms II–X, look for the مُـ prefix; in Form I, expect the مَفْعُول pattern.
explanation.nominal.passiveParticiple.qu: To identify the passive participle (اسم المفعول) of a quadriliteral verb, look for the مُـ prefix — always present. In Form Iq, the pattern is مُفَعْلَل.
explanation.pronoun.base-form: For {pronounLabel}: no prefix or suffix — {arabic} is the stem unmarked.
explanation.pronoun.dropped-prefix: In this form, drop the prefix {elidedPrefix}.
explanation.pronoun.dropped-suffix: In the jussive and subjunctive, drop the nūn {elidedSuffix} ending that would normally appear in the indicative.
explanation.pronoun.prefix-and-suffix: To conjugate for {pronounLabel}: attach {prefix} before the stem and {suffix} after it → {arabic}.
explanation.pronoun.prefix-only: To conjugate for {pronounLabel}: attach {prefix} before the stem → {arabic}.
explanation.pronoun.suffix-only: To conjugate for {pronounLabel}: attach {suffix} after the stem → {arabic}.
explanation.root.assimilated: Watch the initial و — it drops in the Form I active present and imperative, making the verb look biliteral in those cells.
explanation.root.defective-waw: Watch the final و — it surfaces as a long vowel in the present but drops before consonant-initial suffixes.
explanation.root.defective-yaa: Watch the final ي — it surfaces as a long vowel in the present but drops before consonant-initial suffixes.
explanation.root.doubled: The second and third consonants are the same — expect them to contract into a geminate (شَدَّة) when no vowel separates them.
explanation.root.doubly-weak-waw: Two radicals are weak letters; the dominant behavior follows و — expect both hollow and defective patterns to appear.
explanation.root.doubly-weak-yaa: Two radicals are weak letters; the dominant behavior follows ي — expect both hollow and defective patterns to appear.
explanation.root.hamzated-defective-waw: One consonant is hamza and the final radical is و — both behave irregularly; track each one separately.
explanation.root.hamzated-defective-yaa: One consonant is hamza and the final radical is ي — both behave irregularly; track each one separately.
explanation.root.hamzated-doubled: One consonant is hamza and the second and third are the same — expect hamza spelling changes and geminate contraction.
explanation.root.hamzated-hollow-defecti: Three irregularities combine: initial hamza, a hollow middle, and a defective final — each requires separate attention.
explanation.root.hamzated-hollow-waw: One consonant is hamza and the middle radical is و — expect both hamza seat changes and hollow verb behavior.
explanation.root.hamzated-hollow-yaa: One consonant is hamza and the middle radical is ي — expect both hamza seat changes and hollow verb behavior.
explanation.root.hamzated: One consonant is hamza (ء) — its written form changes with surrounding vowels, so check the orthography in each form.
explanation.root.hollow-waw: Watch the middle و — it surfaces as a long vowel but shortens before consonant-initial suffixes; the stem looks different in past vs. present.
explanation.root.hollow-yaa: Watch the middle ي — it surfaces as a long vowel but shortens before consonant-initial suffixes; the stem looks different in past vs. present.
explanation.root.sound: All consonants in {root} are stable — apply the pattern directly, no irregular changes.
explanation.tense-root.final-drops: In the jussive and imperative, forms without a suffix drop the final weak radical entirely: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.final-elides: In the active past, the 3rd-person feminine singular and dual drop the final weak radical silently — the second radical takes a short fatḥa: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.final-isolated: In the 3rd-person masculine singular active past, the final weak radical drops — a long ā ending remains: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.final-lengthens-i: Without a suffix, yā-defective verbs always surface the final ي as long ī: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.final-lengthens-u: Without a suffix, wāw-defective verbs always surface the final و as long ū: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.final-resurfaces: In the active past, before plural and personal suffixes, the final weak radical always resurfaces as a consonant: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.geminate-contract: When no vowel-initial suffix follows, the identical consonants of {root} always contract and geminate: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.geminate-jussive: In the jussive and imperative, both the contracted form (e.g., يَمُدَّ) and the expanded form (يَمْدُدْ) are acceptable.
explanation.tense-root.hamza-seat: Check the hamza’s seat follows the strongest adjacent vowel: kasra → ي, ḍamma → و, fatḥa → ا, no vowel → ء. Result: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.initial-drops: When forming the Form I active present: drop the initial و — {arabic} is the result. It reappears in all other forms.
explanation.tense-root.middle-lengthens-: In the active past, the hollow middle contracts to long ā: {arabic}. Both waw- and yaa-hollow roots use this same vowel.
In the active present, yā-hollow roots always surface the middle as long ī: {arabic}.
In the active present, wāw-hollow roots always surface the middle as long ū: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.middle-passive-aa: In the passive present, both hollow root types surface the middle as long ā: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.middle-passive-ii: In the passive past, both hollow root types contract the middle to long ī: {arabic}.
explanation.tense-root.middle-shortens: In the jussive and imperative, hollow verbs always shorten the middle long vowel and put sukūn on the final consonant: {arabic}.
explanation.tense.active.future: To form the future: prefix سَـ or سَوْفَ to the present indicative — no other change needed.
explanation.tense.active.imperative.elis: To form the imperative: start from the jussive, drop the 2nd-person prefix.
explanation.tense.active.imperative.supp: If two consonants would collide, add alif al-wasl (ا) at the front.
explanation.tense.active.past.form-i: The 3rd person masculine singular (your citation form) is {pastForm}: no prefix, with {pastVowel} on the second consonant.
explanation.tense.active.past: Use the active past for completed actions.
explanation.tense.active.present.indicat: Use the active present indicative for ongoing or habitual actions.
explanation.tense.active.present.jussive: After لَمْ or in conditional structures, use the jussive instead of the indicative. Build it from the present indicative.
explanation.tense.active.present.subjunc: After أَنْ, لَنْ, and similar particles, use the subjunctive instead of the indicative. Build it from the present indicative.
explanation.tense.passive.future: To form the passive future: prefix سَـ or سَوْفَ to the passive present indicative.
explanation.tense.passive.present.indica: Use for ongoing or habitual passive actions.
explanation.tense.passive.present.jussiv: After لَمْ or in conditionals, use the passive jussive.
explanation.tense.passive.present.subjun: After أَنْ, لَنْ, and similar particles, use the passive subjunctive.
explanation.voice.passive.future: Like all passive present-based forms: the prefix vowel is ḍamma and the pre-final vowel is fatḥa.
explanation.voice.passive.past: To recognize the passive past: look for ḍamma on the first syllable and kasra before the final consonant (كُتِبَ — was written).
explanation.voice.passive.present.indica: To recognize the passive present: look for ḍamma on the prefix vowel and fatḥa before the final consonant (يُكْتَبُ — is written).
explanation.voice.passive.present.jussiv: To recognize the passive present: look for ḍamma on the prefix vowel and fatḥa before the final consonant (يُكْتَبُ — is written).
explanation.voice.passive.present.subjun: To recognize the passive present: look for ḍamma on the prefix vowel and fatḥa before the final consonant (يُكْتَبُ — is written).
favourites.empty: Tap ♡ on any verb to save it here.
footer.feedback.body: Muṣarrif is maintained by volunteers. Help us correct mistakes or missing paradigms.
footer.feedback.title: See something off?
formInfo.form1.relationship: Other patterns reshape this base meaning (causative, reflexive, mutual, stative).
formInfo.form10.relationship: It extends Form I or IV; many roots also appear in Form II for a direct causative.
formInfo.form2.relationship: Form II introduces an animate causer — the subject brings about the base action in another entity. Form V turns this causation inward.
formInfo.form3.relationship: The second argument is not a patient but a co-participant — the action flows between two equal agents and is typically ongoing. Form VI makes this mutuality explicit.
formInfo.form4.relationship: Form X often expresses seeking or considering that causative meaning.
formInfo.form5.relationship: The subject is simultaneously causer and caused — it brings about a transformation in itself. This self-causation covers both gradual acquisition and feigned behavior.
formInfo.form6.relationship: Form VI formalizes the co-participation of Form III — each party acts upon the other in a shared, ongoing dynamic.
formInfo.form7.relationship: Think "to become" or "to be acted upon" compared with the bare Form I stem.
formInfo.form8.relationship: Compare with Form I; some roots also use Form VII when the sense is more passive.
formInfo.form9.relationship: They rarely pair with other forms but still anchor to the base Form I meaning.
formInfo.title: Form {form} insights

nominals.title: Nominals
mode.label: Mode
rootInfo.assimilated.description: This is an assimilated root (initial weak), where the first radical is a glide (و or ي). The initial glide typically disappears in the Form I imperfect and imperative, making the verb appear biliteral in those cells.
rootInfo.defective-waw.description: This is a defective root with a wāw (و) as the third radical. It surfaces as a long vowel in the imperfect but drops before consonant-initial suffixes.
rootInfo.defective-yaa.description: This is a defective root with a yā' (ي) as the third radical. It surfaces as a long vowel in the imperfect but drops before consonant-initial suffixes, shortening the preceding vowel.
rootInfo.defective.description: This is a defective root (final weak), where the third radical is a glide (و or ي). It surfaces as a long vowel in the imperfect but deletes before consonant-initial suffixes, causing the preceding vowel to shorten.
rootInfo.doubled.description: This is a doubled root, where the second and third radicals are identical. They merge into a single geminated consonant (šadda) when no vowel separates them, but split apart before consonant-initial suffixes with a short vowel inserted between them.
rootInfo.doubly-weak-waw.description: This is a doubly weak root with two weak radicals, where the dominant weak behavior is waw-like (و).
rootInfo.doubly-weak-yaa.description: This is a doubly weak root with two weak radicals, where the dominant weak behavior is yaa-like (ي).
rootInfo.doubly-weak.description: This is a doubly weak root with two weak radicals, often in the middle and final positions. These roots are relatively rare and tend to combine hollow and defective behavior.
rootInfo.hamzated-defective-waw.descript: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a defective final radical that is specifically و.
rootInfo.hamzated-defective-yaa.descript: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a defective final radical that is specifically ي.
rootInfo.hamzated-defective.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a defective pattern (final weak). The initial radical is a hamza and the final radical is a weak letter (و, ي, or ا).
rootInfo.hamzated-doubled.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a doubled pattern. It contains a hamza and has identical second and third radicals, which often surface as a geminated consonant.
rootInfo.hamzated-hollow-defective.descr: This root combines multiple irregularities: it contains a hamza (ء) at the initial position, has a weak middle radical (و or ي), and has a weak final radical (و, ي, or ا). This combination requires special handling in conjugation.
rootInfo.hamzated-hollow-waw.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a hollow middle radical that is specifically و.
rootInfo.hamzated-hollow-yaa.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a hollow middle radical that is specifically ي.
rootInfo.hamzated-hollow.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a hollow pattern (middle weak). The initial radical is a hamza and the middle radical is a weak letter (و or ي).
rootInfo.hamzated.description: This root contains a hamza (ء) as one of its three radicals. It conjugates like a strong root, but the written form of ء changes depending on the surrounding vowel environment (its "seat").
rootInfo.hollow-waw.description: This is a hollow root with a wāw (و) glide as the second radical. It surfaces as long /ā/ in the imperfect and imperative; in perfective suffix-initial cells, the glide drops and the vowel shortens.
rootInfo.hollow-yaa.description: This is a hollow root with a yā' (ي) glide as the second radical. It surfaces as long /ī/ in the imperfect and imperative; in perfective suffix-initial cells, the glide drops and the vowel shortens.
rootInfo.hollow.description: This is a hollow root (middle weak), where the second radical is a glide (و or ي). The glide surfaces as a long vowel (ā or ī) in the imperfect and imperative, making the perfect and imperfect stems look very different.
rootInfo.sound.description: This is a strong root with all radicals being consonants. Strong roots form the foundation of Arabic verb morphology and can appear in all ten verb forms.
rootInfo.title: Root insights

title: Muṣarrif
tour.step.0.body: Type a root, transliteration, or meaning — Muṣarrif will find what you need.
tour.step.0.title: Find any verb
tour.step.1.body: Filter by form (I–X) or browse the full list — every verb opens its complete conjugation.
tour.step.1.title: Browse verbs
tour.step.2.body: Can’t find the verb here? Build any verb by choosing its root and form.
tour.step.2.title: Build a verb
tour.step.3.body: Switch here to drill conjugation with adaptive exercises — your progress is tracked automatically.
tour.step.3.title: Exercise mode
tour.step.4.body: Return to this tour anytime using the ? button in the header.
